#Text=PART II.
#Text=--FOODS AND THEIR MEDICINAL USES
#Text=
#Text=_Almond._
#Text=
#Text=Almond soup is an excellent substitute for beef-tea for convalescents.
#Text=It is made by simply blanching and pounding a quarter of a pound of
#Text=sweet almonds with half a pint of milk, or vegetable stock.
#Text=Another pint
#Text=of milk or stock is then to be added and the whole warmed.
#Text=After this
#Text=add another pint and a half of stock if the soup is to be a vegetable
#Text=one, or rice water if milk has been used.
#Text=An emulsion of almonds is useful in chest affections.
#Text=It is made by well
#Text=macerating the nuts in a nut butter machine, and mixing with orange or
#Text=lemon juice.
#Text=Almonds should always be blanched, that is, skinned by pouring boiling
#Text=water on the nuts and allowing them to soak for one minute, after which
#Text=the skins are easily removed.
#Text=The latter possess irritating properties.
#Text=Bitter almonds should not be used as a food.
#Text=They contain a poison
#Text=identical with prussic acid.
#Text=_Apple._
#Text=
#Text=It is hardly possible to take up any newspaper or magazine now a days
#Text=without happening on advertisements of patent medicines whose chief
#Text=recommendation is that they "contain phosphorus."
#Text=They are generally
#Text=very expensive, but the reader is assured that they are worth ten times
#Text=the price asked on account of their wonderful properties as nerve and
#Text=brain foods.
#Text=The proprietors of these concoctions seemingly flourish
#Text=like green bay trees and spend many thousands of pounds per annum in
#Text=advertising.
#Text=From which it may be deduced that sufferers from nervous
#Text=exhaustion and brain fag number millions.
#Text=And surely only a sufferer
#Text=from brain fag would suffer himself to be led blindly into wasting his
#Text=money, and still further injuring his health, by buying and swallowing
#Text=drugs about whose properties and effects he knows absolutely nothing.
#Text=How much simpler, cheaper, and more enjoyable to eat apples!
#Text=The apple contains a larger percentage of phosphorus than any other
#Text=fruit or vegetable.
#Text=For this reason it is an invaluable nerve and brain
#Text=food.
#Text=Sufferers from nerve and brain exhaustion should eat at least two
#Text=apples _at the beginning of each meal_.
#Text=At the same time they should
#Text=avoid tea and coffee, and supply their place with barley water or bran
#Text=tea flavoured with lemon juice, or even apple tea.
#Text=Apples are also invaluable to sufferers from the stone or calculus.
#Text=It
#Text=has been observed that in cider countries where the natural unsweetened
#Text=cider is the common beverage, cases of stone are practically unknown.
#Text=Food-reformers do not deduce from this that the drinking of cider is to
#Text=be recommended, but that even better results may be obtained from eating
#Text=the fresh, ripe fruit.
#Text=Apples periodically appear upon the tables of carnivorous feeders in the
#Text=form of apple sauce.
#Text=This accompanies bilious dishes like roast pork and
#Text=roast goose.
#Text=The cook who set this fashion was evidently acquainted with
#Text=the action of the fruit upon the liver.
#Text=All sufferers from sluggish
#Text=livers should eat apples.
#Text=Apples will afford much relief to sufferers from gout.
#Text=The malic acid
#Text=contained in them neutralises the chalky matter which causes the gouty
#Text=patient's sufferings.
#Text=Apples, when eaten ripe and without the addition of sugar, diminish
#Text=acidity in the stomach.
#Text=Certain vegetable salts are converted into
#Text=alkaline carbonates, and thus correct the acidity.
#Text=An old remedy for weak or inflamed eyes is an apple poultice.
#Text=I am told
#Text=that in Lancashire they use rotten apples for this purpose, but
#Text=personally I should prefer them sound.
#Text=A good remedy for a sore or relaxed throat is to take a raw ripe apple
#Text=and scrape it to a fine pulp with a silver teaspoon.
#Text=Eat this pulp by
#Text=the spoonful, very slowly, holding it against the back of the throat as
#Text=long as possible before swallowing.
#Text=A diet consisting chiefly of apples has been found an excellent cure for
#Text=inebriety.
#Text=Health and strength may be fully maintained upon fine
#Text=wholemeal unleavened bread, pure dairy or nut butter, and apples.
#Text=Apple water or apple tea is an excellent drink for fever patients.
#Text=Apples possess tonic properties and provoke appetite for food.
#Text=Hence the
#Text=old-fashioned custom of eating an apple before dinner.
#Text=_Apple Tea._
#Text=
#Text=The following are two good recipes for apple tea:-- (1) Take 2 sound
#Text=apples, wash, but do not peel, and cut into thin slices.
#Text=Add some strips
#Text=of lemon rind.
#Text=Pour on 1 pint of boiling water (distilled).
#Text=Strain when
#Text=cold. (2) Bake 2 apples.
#Text=Pour over them 1 pint boiling water.
#Text=Strain
#Text=when cold.
#Text=_Asparagus._
#Text=
#Text=Asparagus is said to strengthen and develop the artistic faculties.
#Text=It
#Text=also calms palpitation of the heart.
#Text=It is very helpful to rheumatic
#Text=patients on account of its salts of potash.
#Text=It should be steamed, not
#Text=boiled, otherwise part of the valuable salts are lost.
#Text=_Banana._
#Text=
#Text=The banana is invaluable in inflammation of all kinds.
#Text=For this reason
#Text=it is very useful in cases of typhoid fever, gastritis, peritonitis,
#Text=etc., and may constitute the only food allowed for a time.
#Text=Not only does it actually subdue the inflammation of the intestines,
#Text=but, in the opinion of at least one authority, as it consists of 95 per
#Text=cent. nutriment, it does not possess sufficient waste matter to irritate
#Text=the inflamed spots.
#Text=But great care should be taken in its administration.
#Text=The banana should
#Text=be _thoroughly sound and ripe_, and all the stringy portion carefully
#Text=removed.
#Text=It should then be mashed and beaten to a cream.
#Text=In severe cases
#Text=I think it is better to give this neat, but if not liked by the patient
#Text=a little lemon juice, well mixed in, may render it more acceptable.
#Text=It
#Text=may also be taken with fresh cream.
#Text=A friend who has had a very wide experience in illness told me that she
#Text=was once hurriedly sent for at night to a girl suffering from
#Text=peritonitis.
#Text=Not knowing what she might, or might not, find in the way
#Text=of remedies when she arrived at her destination, my friend took with her
#Text=some strong barley water, bananas, and an enema syringe.
#Text=She found the
#Text=girl lying across the bed screaming, obviously in agony.
#Text=First of all my
#Text=friend administered a warm water enema.
#Text=A pint of plain warm water was
#Text=injected first, and after this had come away as much warm water as could
#Text=be got in was injected and then allowed to come away.
#Text=The object of this
#Text=was to thoroughly wash out the bowels.
#Text=Then the barley water was warmed,
#Text=the bananas mashed, beaten to cream, and mixed in with the barley water.
#Text=A soothing nutrient lotion was thus prepared, and as much as the patient
#Text=could bear comfortably was injected in the bowel and retained as long as
#Text=possible.
#Text=The effect was magical.
#Text=The pain subsided, and the patient
#Text=ultimately recovered.
#Text=In the absence of _perfectly_ ripe bananas, baked bananas may be used.
#Text=But, although better than no fruit at all, cooked fruit is never so
#Text=valuable as the fresh fruit, if only the latter be perfectly ripe.
#Text=Bananas should be baked in their skins, and the stringy pieces carefully
#Text=removed before eating.
#Text=From twenty minutes to half an hour's slow
#Text=cooking is required.
#Text=Bananas are excellent food for anæmic persons on account of the iron
#Text=they contain.
#Text=A very palatable way of taking them is with fresh orange
#Text=juice.
#Text=A comparatively old-fashioned remedy, for sprained or bruised places
#Text=that show a tendency to become inflamed is to apply a plaster of banana
#Text=skin.
#Text=_Barley._
#Text=
#Text=Barley is excellent food for the anæmic and nervous on account of its
#Text=richness in iron and phosphoric acid.
#Text=It is also useful in fevers and
#Text=all inflammatory diseases, on account of its soothing properties.
#Text=From
#Text=the earliest times barley water has been the recognised drink of the
#Text=sick.
#Text=_Barley Water._
#Text=
#Text=When using pearl barley for making barley water it must be well washed.
#Text=The fine white dust that adheres to it is most unwholesome.
#Text=For this
#Text=reason the cook is generally directed to first boil the barley for five
#Text=minutes, and throw this water away.
#Text=But in this way some of the valuable
#Text=properties are thrown away with the dirt.
#Text=The best results are obtained
#Text=by well washing it in cold water, but this must be done over and over
#Text=again.
#Text=Half-a-dozen waters will not be too many.
#Text=After the last washing
#Text=the water should be perfectly clear.
#Text=When barley water is being used for curative purposes it should be
#Text=strong.
#Text=The following recipe is an excellent one.
#Text=A ½ pint of barley
#Text=to 2½ pints water (distilled if possible).
#Text=Boil for three hours, or
#Text=until reduced to 2 pints.
#Text=Strain and add 4 teaspoonfuls fresh lemon
#Text=juice.
#Text=Sweeten to taste with pure cane sugar.
#Text=Fine Scotch barley is to be preferred to the pearl barley if it can be
#Text=obtained.
#Text=_Blackberry._
#Text=
#Text=Fresh blackberries are one of the most effectual cures for diarrhoea
#Text=known.
#Text=Mr.
#Text=Broadbent records the case of a child who was cured by eating
#Text=an abundance of blackberries after five doctors had tried all the known
#Text=remedies in vain.
#Text=_Blackberry Tea._
#Text=
#Text=In the absence of the fresh fruit a tea made of blackberry jelly and hot
#Text=water (a large tablespoonful of jelly to half a pint water) will be
#Text=found very useful.
#Text=A teacupful should be taken at short intervals.
#Text=_Blackberry Jelly._
#Text=
#Text=To make blackberry jelly get the first fruit of the season if possible,
#Text=and see that it is ripe or it will yield very little juice.
#Text=Put it into
#Text=the preserving pan, crush it, and allow it to simmer slowly until the
#Text=juice is well drawn out.
#Text=This will take from three-quarters to one hour.
#Text=Strain through a jelly bag, or fine clean muslin doubled will do.
#Text=Then
#Text=measure the juice, and to every pint allow ¾ lb. best cane sugar.
#Text=Return to the pan and boil briskly for from twenty minutes to half an
#Text=hour.
#Text=Stir with a wooden spoon and keep well skimmed.
#Text=To test, put a
#Text=little of the jelly on a cold plate, and if it sets when cold it is
#Text=done.
#Text=While still at boiling point pour into clean, dry, and _hot_
#Text=jars, and tie down with parchment covers immediately.
#Text=_Black Currant._
#Text=
#Text=Black currant tea is one of the oldest of old-fashioned remedies for
#Text=sore throats and colds.
#Text=It is made by pouring half a pint of boiling
#Text=water on to a large tablespoonful of the jelly or jam.
#Text=To make the jelly
#Text=use the same recipe as for blackberry jelly.
#Text=The fresh juice pressed from the fruit is, of course, better than tea
#Text=made from the jelly, but as winter is the season of coughs and colds the
#Text=fruit is least obtainable when most needed.
#Text=_Brazil Nut._
#Text=
#Text=Brazil nuts are excellent for constipation.
#Text=They are also a good
#Text=substitute for suet in puddings.
#Text=Use 5 oz. nuts to 1 lb. flour.
#Text=They
#Text=should be grated in a nut mill or finely chopped.
#Text=_Beans, Peas, and Lentils._
#Text=
#Text=Beans, peas, and lentils are tabooed by the followers of Dr.
#Text=Haig, the
#Text=gout specialist, on account of the belief that they tend to increase the
#Text=secretion of uric acid.
#Text=But this evil propensity is stoutly denied by
#Text=other food-reformers.
#Text=For myself I am inclined to believe that their
#Text=supposed indigestibility, etc., arises from the fact that they are
#Text=generally cooked in hard water.
#Text=They should be cooked in distilled or
#Text=boiled and filtered rain water.
#Text=The addition of lemon juice while
#Text=cooking renders them much more digestible.
#Text=According to Sir Henry Thomson haricot beans are more easily digested
#Text=than meat by most stomachs.
#Text="Consuming weight for weight, the eater
#Text=feels lighter and less oppressed, as a rule, after the leguminous dish;
#Text=while the comparative cost is greatly in favour of the latter."
#Text=Lentils are the most easily digested of all the pulse foods, and
#Text=therefore the most suitable for weakly persons.
#Text=A soup made of
#Text=distilled water and red lentils may be taken twice a week with
#Text=advantage.
#Text=Lentils contain a good percentage of iron, and also
#Text=phosphates.
#Text=_Beet._
#Text=
#Text=The red beet is useful in some diseases of the womb, while the white
#Text=beet is good for the liver.
#Text=It is laxative and diuretic.
#Text=The juice mixed
#Text=with olive oil is also recommended to be applied externally for burns
#Text=and all kinds of running sores.
#Text=_Cabbage._
#Text=
#Text=All the varieties of the colewort tribe, including cabbage, cauliflower,
#Text=brussels-sprouts, broccoli, and curly greens, have been celebrated from
#Text=very ancient times for their curative virtues in pulmonary complaints.
#Text=And Athenian doctors prescribed cabbage for nursing mothers.
#Text=On account
#Text=of the sulphur contained in them cabbages are good for rheumatic
#Text=patients.
#Text=They may be eaten steamed, or, better still, boiled in soft
#Text=water and the broth only taken.
#Text=The ordinary boiled cabbage is an
#Text=indigestible "windy" vegetable, and should never be eaten.
#Text=_Caraway Seed._
#Text=
#Text=Caraway seeds sharpen the vision, promote the secretion of milk, and are
#Text=good against hysterical affections.
#Text=They are also useful in cases of
#Text=colic.
#Text=When used to flavour cakes the seeds should be pounded in a
#Text=mortar, especially if children are to partake thereof.
#Text=When used medicinally 20 grains of the powdered seeds may be taken in a
#Text=wineglassful of hot water.
#Text=But for children half an ounce of the bruised
#Text=seeds are to be infused in cold water for six hours, and from 1 to 3
#Text=teaspoonfuls of this water given.
#Text=A poultice of crushed caraway seeds moistened with hot water is good for
#Text=sprains.
#Text=Caraway seeds are narcotic, and should therefore be used with caution.
#Text=_Carrot._
#Text=
#Text=Carrots are strongly antiseptic.
#Text=They are said to be mentally
#Text=invigorating and nerve restoring.
#Text=They have the reputation of being very
#Text=indigestible on account of the fact that they are generally boiled, not
#Text=steamed.
#Text=When used medicinally it is best to take the fresh, raw juice.
#Text=This is easily obtained by grating the carrot finely on a common penny
#Text=bread grater, and straining and pressing the pulp thus obtained.
#Text=Raw carrot juice, or a raw carrot eaten fasting, will expel worms.
#Text=The
#Text=cooked carrot is useless for this purpose.
#Text=A poultice of fresh carrot pulp will heal ulcers.
#Text=Fresh carrot juice is also good for consumptives on account of the large
#Text=amount of sugar it contains.
#Text=Carrots are very good for gouty subjects and for derangements of the
#Text=liver.
#Text=_Celery._
#Text=
#Text=Celery is almost a specific for rheumatism, gout, and nervous
#Text=indigestion.
#Text=The most useful plants for this purpose are small, not too
#Text=rapidly grown nor very highly manured.
#Text=It may be eaten raw, or steamed, or in soup.
#Text=Strong celery broth
#Text=flavoured with parsley is excellent.
#Text=_Cresses._
#Text=
#Text=All the cresses are anti-scorbutic, that is, useful against the scurvy.
#Text=The ancient Greeks also believed them to be good for the brain.
#Text=The ordinary "mustard and cress" of our salads is good for rheumatic
#Text=patients, while the water-cress is valuable in cases of tubercular
#Text=disease.
#Text=Anæmic patients may also eat freely of it on account of the
#Text=iron it contains.
#Text=Care should be taken, however, from whence it is
#Text=procured, as a disease peculiar to sheep but communicable to man may be
#Text=carried by it.
#Text=It should not be gathered from streams running through
#Text=meadows inhabited by sheep.
#Text=_Chestnut._
#Text=
#Text=Chestnuts, when cooked, are valuable food for persons with weak
#Text=digestive powers.
#Text=They should be put on the fire in a saucepan of cold
#Text=water and cooked for twenty minutes from the time the water first boils.
#Text=John Evelyn, F.R.S., a seventeenth century writer, says of them: "They
#Text=are a lusty and masculine food for rustics at all times, and of better
#Text=nourishment for husbandmen than cole and rusty bacon, yea, or beans to
#Text=boot."
#Text=_Cinnamon._
#Text=
#Text=Cinnamon is a very old-fashioned remedy for soothing the pain of
#Text=internal or unbroken cancer.
#Text=One prescription is the following: Take
#Text=1 lb. of Ceylon sticks.
#Text=Simmer in a closed vessel with 1 quart of water
#Text=until the liquid is reduced to 1 pint.
#Text=Pour off without straining, and
#Text=shake or stir well before taking.
#Text=Take half a pint every twenty-four
#Text=hours.
#Text=Divide into small doses and take regularly.
#Text=Cinnamon has a powerful influence over disease germs, but care must be
#Text=taken to obtain it pure.
#Text=It is often adulterated with cassia.
#Text=Cinnamon tea may be taken with advantage in cases of consumption,
#Text=influenza, and pneumonia.
#Text=_Cocoanut._
#Text=
#Text=Cocoanut is an old and very efficacious remedy for intestinal worms of
#Text=all kinds.
#Text=A tablespoonful of freshly-ground cocoanut should be taken at
#Text=breakfast until the cure is complete.
#Text=The dessicated cocoanut is useless
#Text=for curative purposes.
#Text=_Coffee._
#Text=
#Text=Coffee is a most powerful antiseptic, and therefore very useful as a
#Text=disinfectant.
#Text=It has been used as a specific against cholera with
#Text=marvellous results, and is useful in all cases of intestinal
#Text=derangement.
#Text=But only the pale-roasted varieties should be taken, as the
#Text=roasting develops the poisonous, irritating properties.
#Text=There is
#Text=_always_ danger in the roasting of grains or berries on account of the
#Text=new substances that may be developed.
#Text=I do not recommend coffee as a beverage, but as a medicine.
#Text=_Date._
#Text=
#Text=The nourishing properties of dates are well known.
#Text=They are easily
#Text=digested, and for this reason are often recommended to consumptive
#Text=patients.
#Text=According to Dr.
#Text=Fernie half a pound of dates and half a pint of new
#Text=milk will make a satisfying repast for a person engaged in sedentary
#Text=work.
#Text=_Elderberry._
#Text=
#Text=The elderberry has fallen into neglect of late years, owing to the lazy
#Text=and disastrous modern habit of substituting the mineral drugs of the
#Text=chemist for the home-made vegetable remedies of our grandmothers.
#Text=Nevertheless, the elderberry is one of the most ancient and tried of
#Text=medicines, held in such great esteem in Germany that, according to the
#Text=German folk-lore, men should take off their hats in the presence of an
#Text=elder-tree.
#Text=In Denmark there is a legend to the effect that the trees
#Text=are under the protection of a being known as the Elder-Mother, who has
#Text=been immortalised in one of the fairy tales of Hans Andersen.
#Text=The berries of the elder-tree are not palatable enough to be used as a
#Text=common article of food, but in the days when nearly every garden boasted
#Text=its elder-tree few housewives omitted to make elderberry wine in due
#Text=season.
#Text=It is not permitted to "food-reformers" to make "wine," but those
#Text=readers who are fortunate enough to possess an elder-tree might well
#Text=preserve the juice of the berries against winter coughs and colds.
#Text=_Preserved Fruit Juice._
#Text=
#Text=The following is E. and B.
#Text=May's recipe for preserving fruit juice.
#Text=Put
#Text=the fruit into a preserving-pan, crush it and allow it to simmer slowly
#Text=until the juice is well drawn out.
#Text=This will take about an hour.
#Text=Press
#Text=out the juice and strain through a jelly-bag until quite clear.
#Text=Put the
#Text=juice back into the pan, and to every quart add a quarter of a pound of
#Text=best cane sugar.
#Text=Stir until dissolved.
#Text=Put the juice into clean, dry
#Text=bottles.
#Text=Stand the bottles in a pan of hot water, and when the latter
#Text=has come to the boil allow the bottles to remain in the boiling water
#Text=for fifteen minutes.
#Text=The idea is to bring the juice inside the bottles
#Text=to boiling point just before sealing up, but not to boil it.
#Text=See that
#Text=the bottles are _full_.
#Text=Cork _immediately_ on taking out of the pan,
#Text=and then seal up.
#Text=To seal mix a little plaster of Paris with water and
#Text=spread it well over the cork.
#Text=Let it come a little below the cork so as
#Text=to exclude all air.
#Text=The juice of the elderberry is famous for promoting perspiration, hence
#Text=its efficacy in the cure of colds.
#Text=Two tablespoonfuls should be taken at
#Text=bed-time in a tumbler of hot water.
#Text=The juice of the elderberry is excellent in fevers, and is also said to
#Text=promote longevity.
#Text=_Elderberry Poultice._
#Text=
#Text="The leaves of the elder, boiled until they are soft, with a little
#Text=linseed oil added thereto," laid upon a scarlet cloth and applied, as
#Text=hot as it can be borne, to piles, has been said to be an infallible
#Text=remedy.
#Text=Each time this poultice gets cold it must be renewed for "the
#Text=space of an hour."
#Text=At the end of this time the final dressing is to be
#Text="bound on," and the patient "put warm to bed."
#Text=If necessary the whole
#Text=operation is to be repeated; but the writer assures us that "this hath
#Text=not yet failed at the first dressing to cure the disease."
#Text=If any reader
#Text=desires to try the experiment I would suggest that the leaves be steamed
#Text=rather than boiled, and pure olive oil used in the place of linseed oil.
#Text=It must also be remembered that no outward application can be expected
#Text=to effect a permanent cure, since the presence of piles indicates an
#Text=effort of Nature to clear out some poison from the system.
#Text=But if this
#Text=expulsion is assisted by appropriate means the pain may well be
#Text=alleviated by external applications.
#Text=(Pepper should be avoided by
#Text=sufferers from piles.)
#Text=_Fig._
#Text=
#Text=A "lump of figs" laid on the boil of King Hezekiah, as recorded in 2
#Text=Kings xx. 7, brought about that monarch's recovery.
#Text=The figs used were
#Text=doubtless ripe figs, not the dried figs of our grocers.
#Text="This fruit," says Dr.
#Text=Fernie, "is soft, easily digested, and corrective
#Text=of strumous disease."
#Text=The large blue fig may be grown in England, in the
#Text=milder parts and under a warm wall.
#Text=The fresh figs were rarely seen at
#Text=one time outside of the large "high-class" fruit shops, but for the last
#Text=year or two I have seen them peddled in the streets of London like
#Text=apples and oranges in due season.
#Text=Green figs (not unripe) were commonly eaten by Roman gladiators, which
#Text=is surely a sufficient tribute to the fruit's strength-giving qualities.
#Text=The best way of preparing dried figs for eating is to wash them very
#Text=quickly in warm water, and steam for twenty minutes or until tender.
#Text=_Grape._
#Text=
#Text=The special value of the grape lies in the fact that it is a very quick
#Text=repairer of bodily waste, the grape sugar being taken immediately into
#Text=the circulation without previous digestion.
#Text=For this reason is grape
#Text=juice the best possible food for fever patients, consumptives, and all
#Text=who are in a weak and debilitated condition.
#Text=The grapes should be well
#Text=chewed, the juice and pulp swallowed, and the skin and stones rejected.
#Text=In countries where the grape cure is practised, consumptive patients are
#Text=fed on the sweeter varieties of grape, while those troubled with liver
#Text=complaints, acid gout, or other effects of over-feeding, take the less
#Text=sweet kinds.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie deprecates the use of grapes for the ordinary gouty or
#Text=rheumatic patient, but with all due deference to that learned authority,
#Text=I do not believe the fruit exists that is not beneficial to the gouty
#Text=person.
#Text=One of the most gouty and rheumatic people I know, a vegetarian
#Text=who certainly never over-feeds himself, derives great benefit from a few
#Text=days' almost exclusive diet of grapes.
#Text=Cream of tartar, a potash salt obtained from the crust formed upon
#Text=bottles and casks by grape juice when it is undergoing fermentation in
#Text=the process of becoming wine, is often used as a medicine.
#Text=It has been
#Text=cited as an infallible specific in cases of smallpox, but I do not
#Text=recommend its use, as it probably gets contaminated with other
#Text=substances during the process of manufacture.
#Text=In any case its value
#Text=cannot be compared with the fresh, ripe fruit.
#Text=I have little doubt but
#Text=that an exclusive diet of grapes, combined with warmth, proper bathing,
#Text=and the absence of drugs, would suffice to cure the most malignant case
#Text=of smallpox.
#Text=Sufferers from malaria may use grapes with great benefit.
#Text=For this
#Text=purpose the grapes, with the skins and stones, should be well pounded in
#Text=a mortar and allowed to stand for three hours.
#Text=The juice should then be
#Text=strained off and taken.
#Text=Or persons with good teeth may eat the grapes,
#Text=including the skins and stones, if they thoroughly macerate the latter.
#Text=In the absence of fresh grapes raisin-tea is a restoring and nourishing
#Text=drink.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie notes that it is of the same proteid value as milk, if
#Text=made in the proportions given below.
#Text=It is much more easily digested
#Text=than milk, and therefore of great use in gastric complaints.
#Text=Sufferers
#Text=from chronic gastritis could not do better than make raisin-tea their
#Text=sole drink, and bananas their only food for a time.
#Text=_Raisin Tea._
#Text=
#Text=To make raisin-tea, take half a pound of good raisins and wash well, but
#Text=quickly, in lukewarm water.
#Text=Cut up roughly and put into the
#Text=old-fashioned beef-tea jar with a quart of _distilled_ or boiled and
#Text=filtered _rain_ water.
#Text=Cook for four hours, or until the liquid is
#Text=reduced to 1 pint.
#Text=Scald a fine hair sieve and press through it all
#Text=except the skins and stones.
#Text=If desired a little lemon juice may be
#Text=added.
#Text=_Gooseberry._
#Text=
#Text=The juice of green gooseberries "cureth all inflammations," while the
#Text=red gooseberry is good for bilious subjects.
#Text=But it has been said that
#Text=gooseberries are not good for melancholy persons.
#Text=Gooseberries are an excellent "spring medicine."
#Text=_Lavender._
#Text=
#Text=It is very much to be regretted that the nerve-soothing vegetable
#Text=perfumes of our grandmothers have been superseded, for the most part, by
#Text=the cheap mineral products of the laboratory.
#Text=Scents really prepared
#Text=from the flowers that give them their names are expensive to make, and
#Text=consequently high-priced.
#Text=The cheap scents are all mineral concoctions,
#Text=and their use is more or less injurious.
#Text=A penny-worth of dried lavender
#Text=flowers in a muslin bag is even cheaper to buy, inoffensive to
#Text=smell--which is more than can be said of cheap manufactured scents--and
#Text=possesses medicinal properties.
#Text=Lavender flowers were formerly used for their curative virtues in all
#Text=disorders of the head and nerves.
#Text=An oil, prepared by infusing the crushed lavender flowers in olive oil,
#Text=is recommended for anointing palsied limbs, and at one time a spirit was
#Text=prepared from lavender flowers which was known as "palsy drops."
#Text=A tea made with hot water and lavender tops will relieve the headache
#Text=that comes from fatigue.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie advises 1 dessertspoonful per day of pure lavender water for
#Text=eczema.
#Text=The scent of lavender will keep away flies, fleas, and moths.
#Text=_Lemon._
#Text=
#Text=Lemons are invaluable in cases of gout, malaria, rheumatism, and scurvy.
#Text=They are also useful in fevers and liver complaints.
#Text=I have found the juice of one lemon taken in a little hot water remove
#Text=dizzy feelings in the head, accompanied by specks and lights dancing
#Text=before the eyes, consequent upon the liver being out of order, in half
#Text=an hour.
#Text=The juice of a lemon in hot water may be taken night and morning with
#Text=advantage by sufferers from rheumatism.
#Text=In the "lemon cure" for gout and
#Text=rheumatism, the patients begin with one lemon per day and increase the
#Text=quantity until they arrive at a dozen or more.
#Text=But I think this is
#Text=carrying it to excess.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie recommends the juice of one lemon
#Text=mixed with an equal proportion of hot water, to be taken pretty
#Text=frequently, in cases of rheumatic fever.
#Text=A prescription for malaria, given in the _Lancet_, is the following:
#Text="Take a full-sized lemon, cut it in thin transverse slices, rind and
#Text=all, boil these down in an earthenware jar containing a pint and a half
#Text=of water, until the decoction is reduced to half a pint.
#Text=They
#Text=cost anything from six or seven shillings.
#Text=The daily allowance of nuts
#Text=may be thoroughly macerated and eaten with fruit in the place of cream.
#Text=Ordinary people may use a nut-mill, which flakes, not macerates, the
#Text=nuts.
#Text=But people with bad teeth and a weak digestion will do better to
#Text=invest in a nut butter machine.
#Text=I may add that the nuts will not
#Text=macerate properly unless they are crisp, and to this end they must be
#Text=put in a warm oven for a short time, just before grinding.
#Text=I have found
#Text=new, English-grown walnuts crisp enough without this preparation.
#Text=But if
#Text=the nuts are _not_ crisp enough they will simply clog the machine.
#Text=Now to our nuts!
#Text=Almonds are the most nourishing.
#Text=Next in order come
#Text=walnuts, hazel or cob nuts, and Brazil nuts.
#Text=The proteid value of these
#Text=three does not differ much.
#Text=After these come the chestnut and cocoanut,
#Text=and lastly we have the pine kernel.
#Text=Speaking very roughly, we may liken
#Text=walnuts, hazel nuts, and Brazil nuts to beef for flesh and
#Text=muscle-forming value, while pine kernels correspond more nearly to fish.
#Text=Almonds are nearly double the value of beef.
#Text=_Nut Cream._
#Text=
#Text=Doctor Fernie recommends the following nut-cream for brain-workers.
#Text=Pound in a mortar, or mince finely, 3 blanched almonds, 2 walnuts, 2
#Text=ounces of pine kernels.
#Text=Steep overnight in orange or lemon juice.
#Text=It should be made fresh daily, and may be used in place of butter.
#Text=_Oat._
#Text=
#Text=The oat is generally cited as the most nourishing of all the cereals,
#Text=and a good nerve food.
#Text=The fine oatmeal gruel of our grandmothers has
#Text=gone almost entirely out of fashion, but its use might be revived with
#Text=advantage.
#Text=Like wheat, it is a complete food.
#Text=A good preparation of
#Text=groats (ground oats from which the husk has been entirely removed) may
#Text=be taken by those who find other preparations indigestible.
#Text=Some persons seem unable to take oatmeal, its use being followed by a
#Text=skin eruption.
#Text=This is supposed to be due to a special constituent
#Text=called "avenin," the existence of which, however, is denied by some
#Text=authorities.
#Text=There is little doubt but that persons of weak digestive powers and
#Text=sedentary habits cannot digest porridge comfortably.
#Text=In any case
#Text=quickly-cooked porridge is an abomination.
#Text=_Olive._
#Text=
#Text=The chief use of the olive, at least in this country, consists in the
#Text=oil expressed from it.
#Text=Unfortunately our so-called olive oil is
#Text=generally cotton-seed oil.
#Text=Captain Diamond of San Francisco, aged 111,
#Text=and the oldest living athlete in the world, attributes much of his
#Text=health to the use of olive oil.
#Text=But he lays great stress upon the
#Text=importance of obtaining it pure.
#Text=Cotton-seed oil consists partly of an
#Text=indigestible gum, and its continued ingestion tends to produce kidney
#Text=trouble and heart failure.
#Text=A simple test for purity is to use, the suspected sample for oiling
#Text=floors or furniture.
#Text=If pure, it will leave a beautiful polish minus
#Text=grease.
#Text=But if it contains cotton-seed oil, part of it will evaporate,
#Text=leaving the gummy portion behind.
#Text=When pure olive oil is shaken in a half-filled bottle, the bubbles
#Text=formed thereby rapidly disappear, but if the sample is adulterated the
#Text=bubbles continue some time before they burst.
#Text=Pure olive oil is pale and a greenish yellow.
#Text=If equal volumes of strong nitric acid (this may be obtained from any
#Text=chemist) and olive oil are mixed together and shaken in a flask the
#Text=resulting product has a greenish or orange tinge which remains unchanged
#Text=after standing for ten minutes.
#Text=But if cotton-seed oil is present, the
#Text=mixture is reddish in colour, and becomes brown or black on standing.
#Text=Olive oil is slightly laxative, and therefore useful to sufferers from
#Text=constipation.
#Text=It is also an excellent vermifuge.
#Text=Olive oil has been used with great success in the treatment of gall
#Text=stones.
#Text=A Dr.
#Text=Rosenberg reported that of twenty-one cases treated by
#Text="the ingestion of a considerable quantity of olive oil, only two failed
#Text=of complete recovery."
#Text=_Onion._
#Text=
#Text=The uses of the onion are many and varied.
#Text=Fresh onion juice promotes
#Text=perspiration, relieves constipation and bronchitis, induces sleep, is
#Text=good for cases of scurvy and sufferers from lead colic.
#Text=It is also
#Text=excellent for bee and wasp stings.
#Text=Onions are noted for their nerve-soothing properties.
#Text=They are also
#Text=beautifiers of the complexion.
#Text=But moderation must be observed in their
#Text=use or they are apt to disagree.
#Text=Not everyone can digest onions,
#Text=although I believe them to be more easily digested raw than cooked.
#Text=A raw onion may be rubbed on unbroken chilblains with good results.
#Text=If
#Text=broken, the onion should be roasted.
#Text=The heart of a roasted onion placed
#Text=in the ear is an old-fashioned remedy for earache.
#Text=Raw onions are a powerful antiseptic.
#Text=They also attract disease germs to
#Text=themselves, and for this reason may be placed in a sickroom with
#Text=advantage.
#Text=Needless to say, they should afterwards be burnt or buried.
#Text=Culpeper, the ancient herbalist, says that they "draw corruption unto
#Text=them."
#Text=It is possibly for this reason that the Vedanta forbids them to
#Text=devout Hindoos.
#Text=Garlic possesses the same properties as the onion, but in a very much
#Text=stronger degree.
#Text=Leeks are very much milder than the onion.
#Text=_Onion Juice._
#Text=
#Text=The following prescription is excellent for sufferers from bronchitis or
#Text=coughs: Slice a Spanish onion; lay the slices in a basin and sprinkle
#Text=well with pure cane sugar.
#Text=Cover the basin tightly and leave for twelve
#Text=hours.
#Text=After this time the basin should contain a quantity of juice.
#Text=Give a teaspoonful every now and then until relief is afforded.
#Text=If too
#Text=much be taken it may induce headache and vomiting.
#Text=_Onion Poultice._
#Text=
#Text=An excellent poultice for the chest may be made by placing one or two
#Text=English onions in a muslin bag and pounding them to a pulp.
#Text=This should
#Text=be renewed every three or four hours, and the chest washed.
#Text=I have been
#Text=told that, at the age of six weeks old, I was saved from dying of
#Text=bronchitis by such an onion poultice applied to the soles of my feet.
#Text=_Orange._
#Text=
#Text=The orange possesses most of the virtues of the lemon, but in a modified
#Text=form.
#Text=But it has the advantage of being more palatable.
#Text=The juice of oranges has been observed to exert such a beneficial
#Text=influence on the blood as to prevent and cure influenza.
#Text=Taken freely
#Text=while the attack is on they seemingly prevent the pneumonia that so
#Text=often follows.
#Text=By far the quickest way to overcome influenza is to
#Text=subsist solely on oranges for three or four days.
#Text=Hot distilled water
#Text=may be taken in addition.
#Text=The peel of the bitter Seville orange is an excellent tonic and remedy
#Text=in cases of malaria and ague.
#Text=A drink may be prepared from it according
#Text=to the prescription under the heading "Lemon."
#Text=The "orange cure" is used with great success for consumptive patients,
#Text=for chest affections of all kinds, for asthma, and some stomach
#Text=complaints.
#Text=Oranges are taken freely at every meal.
#Text=The "navel" kind are
#Text=generally used.
#Text=Herbalists sell dried orange pips to be crushed to a powder and taken in
#Text=the proportion of 1 teaspoonful to a cup of hot water.
#Text=This is a
#Text=harmless sedative, and useful in hysterical affections.
#Text=_Marmalade Tonic._
#Text=
#Text=A drink made with half a pint of hot water poured over a tablespoonful
#Text=of good, home-made marmalade will often give relief in cases of
#Text=neuralgia and pains in the head.
#Text=_Parsley._
#Text=
#Text=Parsley is useful in cases of menstrual obstruction and diseases of the
#Text=kidneys.
#Text=The bruised leaves applied to the breasts of nursing mothers
#Text=are said to cure painful lumps and threatened abscess.
#Text=It may also be
#Text=taken with advantage by cancerous patients.
#Text=In all these cases parsley
#Text=may be taken in the form of a soup, in common use among members of the
#Text=Physical Regeneration Society, which consists of onions, tomatoes,
#Text=celery, and parsley, stewed together in distilled water.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie remarks that when uncooked parsley has been eaten to excess
#Text=it has been observed to produce epilepsy in certain bodily systems.
#Text=The
#Text=oil of parsley has also been found useful in cases of epilepsy.
#Text=This
#Text=would naturally follow on the homeopathic principle of similars.
#Text=_Pear._
#Text=
#Text=The pear possesses most of the virtues of the apple.
#Text=But, unlike the
#Text=latter, it is credited with producing a constipating effect if eaten
#Text=without its skin.
#Text=In an old recipe book I found the following tribute to
#Text=Bergamot pears.
#Text=The writer says: "I had for some years been afflicted
#Text=with the usual symptoms of the stone in the bladder, when meeting with
#Text=Dr.
#Text=induced on his recommendation to try Bergamot pears, a dozen or more
#Text=every day with the rind, when in less than a week I observed a large red
#Text=flake in my urine, which, on a slight touch, crumbled into the finest
#Text=powder, and this was the same for several succeeding days.
#Text=It is ten
#Text=years since I made the experiment, and I have been quite free from any
#Text=complaints of that nature ever since.
#Text=The pears were of the small sort
#Text=and full of knots."
#Text=is liquid.
#Text=Hard biscuits will do instead of nuts, although an uncooked
#Text=food like the nut is the better.
#Text=But whatever is taken must be
#Text="Fletcherised," that is, chewed and chewed and chewed until it is all
#Text=reduced to liquid.
#Text=Pea nuts contain a good deal of oil, and for this reason are recommended
#Text=for consumptives.
#Text=They are the cheapest nuts to buy, for the reason that
#Text=they are not really nuts but beans.
#Text=_Pine-apple._
#Text=
#Text=Pine-apple juice is the specific for diphtheria.
#Text=This seems to have
#Text=been first brought to the notice of Europeans by the fact that negroes
#Text=living round about the swamps of Louisiana were observed to use it with
#Text=great success.
#Text=A writer who records this says: "The patient should be
#Text=forced to swallow the juice.

412-18	39545-39546	.	_	_	_	

#Text=This fluid is of so pungent and corrosive a
#Text=nature that it cuts out the diphtheria mucous and causes it to
#Text=disappear.

_	_	_	

#Text=The above direction looks satisfactory enough on paper, and it is
#Text=eminently cheering to read of how the pine-apple juice causes the
#Text=diphtheria mucous to disappear, but anyone who knows anything about
#Text=diphtheria knows that to force" a diphtheria patient to swallow is more

column1: #Text=easily written about than accomplished.
#Text=Fortunately I have been able to
#Text=obtain the following explicit directions from an experienced nurse and
#Text=mother:
#Text=
#Text=The pine-apple should be cut up and well pounded in a mortar.
#Text=The juice
#Text=must then be pressed out and strained through well-scalded muslin.
#Text=The
#Text=patient's mouth must be washed out with warm water.
#Text=The juice may now be
#Text=given with a silver teaspoon.
#Text=It is possible that the patient may be
#Text=quite unable to swallow any of it.
#Text=If this be so, the juice will serve
#Text=as a mouth and throat wash.
#Text=It will gradually dissolve the membrane, and
#Text=enable it to be scraped gently away with the spoon.
#Text=The juice should be
#Text=given, and the throat scraped as far down as the nurse can reach, as
#Text=often as the patient can bear it.
#Text=The time will come, sooner or later,
#Text=when the juice is swallowed.
#Text=No other food should be given.
#Text=The nurse
#Text=may have to work away for some hours before any juice is swallowed, but
#Text=my friend assures me that if the scraping be done gently and skilfully,
#Text=even children will bear it patiently.
#Text=Only a silver or bone spoon should
#Text=be used, and, needless to say, it must be well scalded in boiling water
#Text=in the intervals of using.
#Text=It is a remarkable fact that while pine-apple juice exercises this
#Text=remarkable corrosive power upon diseased mucous, its effect upon the
#Text=most delicate, healthy membrane is absolutely harmless.
#Text=I have seen
#Text=sweet pine-apple juice given to six-months-old babies as a supplement to
#Text=the mother's milk, with excellent results.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Hillier, writing in the _Herald of Health_ in 1897, says "Sliced
#Text=pine-apples, laid in pure honey for a day or two, when used in
#Text=moderation, will relieve the human being from chronic impaction of the
#Text=bowels, reestablish peristaltic motion, and induce perfect digestion."
#Text="A slice of fresh pine-apple," writes Dr.
#Text=Fernie, "is about as wise a
#Text=thing as one can take by way of dessert after a substantial meal."
#Text=This
#Text=is because fresh pine-apple juice has been found to act upon animal food
#Text=in very much the same way that the gastric juice acts within the
#Text=stomach.
#Text=But vegetarians should eat fresh fruit at the beginning of
#Text=meals rather than at the end.
#Text=The pine-apple is useful in all ordinary cases of sore-throat.
#Text=One pine-apple of average size should yield half a pint of juice.
#Text=Tinned or cooked pine-apple is useless for curative purposes.
#Text=_Pine Kernel._
#Text=
#Text=Pine kernels are recommended to those who find other nuts difficult to
#Text=digest.
#Text=They are the most easily digested of all the nuts.
#Text=They are
#Text=often used for cooking in the place of suet, being very oily.
#Text=_Plum, Prune._
#Text=
#Text=The disfavour with which "stone fruits," especially plums, are generally
#Text=regarded owes its being to the fact that they are too often eaten when
#Text=unripe.
#Text=When ripe, they are as wholesome as any other fruit.
#Text=Unripe they
#Text=provoke choleraic diarrhoea.
#Text=The prune, a variety of dried plum, has been recommended as a remedy
#Text=against viciousness and irritability.
#Text=An American doctor declares that
#Text=there is a certain medicinal property in the prune which acts directly
#Text=upon the nervous system, and that is where the evil passions have their
#Text=seat.
#Text=He reports that he tried the experiment of including prunes in the
#Text=meals of the vicious, intractable youths of a reformatory, and that by
#Text=the end of a week they were peaceable as lambs.
#Text=Most writers who comment
#Text=on this seem to suggest that any fruit which is mildly aperient would
#Text=produce the same effect.
#Text=But the mother of a large family tells me that
#Text=she has observed that prunes seem to possess a soothing property that is
#Text=all their own.
#Text=_Prune Tea._
#Text=
#Text=Prune tea is an excellent drink for irritable persons.
#Text=It is made as
#Text=follows: To every pint of washed prunes allow 1 quart of distilled
#Text=water.
#Text=Soak the prunes all night, and afterwards simmer to rags in the
#Text=same water.
#Text=Strain, and flavour with lemon juice if desired.
#Text=_Potato._
#Text=
#Text=The potato is a cheap and homely remedy against gout, scurvy, and
#Text=rickets.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Lambe tells how he cured a case of scurvy solely with raw
#Text=potatoes.
#Text=One of the favourite dishes of that good old doctor was a
#Text=salad composed of sliced raw potatoes and olive oil.
#Text=In order to preserve the medicinal properties of potatoes when cooked,
#Text=they must always be steamed in their jackets.
#Text=The skin may be removed
#Text=before eating, but care should be taken not to allow a particle of the
#Text=potato to adhere to it.
#Text=The valuable potash salts chiefly lie just under
#Text=the skin.
#Text=A raw potato scraped or powdered to a pulp is an excellent remedy for
#Text=burns and scalds.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie recommends the following decoction with which to bathe the
#Text=swollen and inflamed joints of rheumatic sufferers.
#Text=Take 1 lb.
#Text=potatoes, cut each into four, but do not peel them.
#Text=Boil in 2 pints of
#Text=water until stewed down to 1 pint.
#Text=Strain, and use the liquid.
#Text=Eaten to excess potatoes are apt to cause dullness and laziness.
#Text=_Radish._
#Text=
#Text=The radish is commonly cited as indigestible, but for all that it is
#Text=commended by old writers as a potent remedy for stone.
#Text=If not too old,
#Text=well masticated, and eaten at the beginning of a meal, I do not think it
#Text=is more indigestible than the majority of vegetables.
#Text=A syrup made with the juice expressed from pounded radishes and cane
#Text=sugar is recommended for rheumatism, bronchial troubles, whooping-cough,
#Text=and pustular eruptions.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Fernie notes that the black radish is especially useful against
#Text=whooping-cough, probably by reason of its volatile, sulphureted oil.
#Text="It is employed in Germany for this purpose by cutting off the top, and
#Text=then making a hole within the root, which hole is filled with treacle,
#Text=or honey, and allowed to stand thus for two or three days; afterwards a
#Text=teaspoonful of the medicated liquid is to be given two or three times in
#Text=the day, with a dessertspoonful of water, when required."
#Text=I am not acquainted with the "black radish," but mothers might do worse,
#Text=in cases of whooping-cough, than give their children the juice of
#Text=pounded radishes mixed with pure honey.
#Text=_Raspberry._
#Text=
#Text=Raspberries are excellent against the scurvy, and, like the blackberry,
#Text=good for relaxed bowels.
#Text=They are a very wholesome fruit, and should be
#Text=given to those who have "weak and queasy stomachs."
#Text=_Rice._
#Text=
#Text=The chief medicinal value of rice lies in the quickness with which it
#Text=is digested.
#Text=One authority says that "it can be taken four times a day
#Text=and the patient still get twenty hours' rest."
#Text=It is consequently of
#Text=great value in digestive and intestinal troubles.
#Text=But it should be
#Text=_unpolished_, otherwise it is an ill-balanced, deficient food.
#Text=It should
#Text=likewise be boiled in only just enough soft water to be absorbed during
#Text=the cooking.
#Text=One cup of rice should be put on in a double saucepan with
#Text=three cups of cold water and tightly covered.
#Text=When the water is all
#Text=absorbed the rice will be cooked.
#Text=The large-grained, unpolished rice sold at "Food-Reform" stores at 3d.
#Text=per lb. absorbs the water and cooks much more easily than a smaller
#Text=variety sold at 2d.
#Text=I have found the latter most unsatisfactory.
#Text=_Rhubarb._
#Text=
#Text=Rhubarb is a wholesome and cooling spring vegetable, and may well take
#Text=the place of cooked fruit when the latter is scarce.
#Text=But it is
#Text=generally forbidden to rheumatic and gouty patients on account of its
#Text=oxalic acid.
#Text=This oxalic acid is supposed to combine with the lime in
#Text=the blood of the gouty person, and to form crystals of oxalate of lime,
#Text=which are eliminated by the kidneys.
#Text=At the same time the general health
#Text=suffers.
#Text="Dr.
#Text=instances in which an oxalate of lime kidney attack has followed the use
#Text=of garden rhubarb in a tart or pudding, likewise of sorrel in a salad,
#Text=particularly when at the same time the patient has been drinking hard
#Text=water.

column3:
_	_	_	

#Text=I think the moral of the above is: Do not drink hard water."

column1: #Text=Especially do not cook fruit and vegetables in hard water.
#Text=They are
#Text=nearly always rendered indigestible by such a process, and
#Text="vegetarianism," not the hard water, is often blamed for the sufferings
#Text=of the consumers.
#Text=Rhubarb is apt to be over-valued as a "spring medicine" on account of
#Text=its association with the Turkey rhubarb of _materia medica_.
#Text=It should
#Text=be thoroughly ripe before eating.
#Text=I am _not_ recommending Turkey rhubarb.
#Text=_Sage._
#Text=
#Text=Sage is said to promote longevity, to quicken the senses and memory, and
#Text=to strengthen the nerves.
#Text=Sage tea is recommended for pulmonary consumption and for excessive
#Text=perspiration of the feet.
#Text=A teaspoonful of dried sage, or rather more if
#Text=the fresh leaves be used, is steeped in half a pint of water for
#Text=twenty-four hours.
#Text=A teacupful is to be taken night and morning.
#Text=Sage, like so many of the fragrant herbs, is antiseptic.
#Text=_Strawberry._
#Text=
#Text=The strawberry is exceptionally wholesome on account of its being so
#Text=easily digested.
#Text=It is recommended for gout, rheumatism, and the stone.
#Text=Also for anæmic patients on account of the iron it contains.
#Text=H.
#Text=Benjafield, M.B., advises anæmic girls to take 1 quart of
#Text=strawberries per day, and when these are not obtainable several ripe
#Text=bananas.
#Text=_Spinach._
#Text=
#Text=Professor Bunge declared that iron should never be taken in its mineral
#Text=form, but that those who are in need of an iron tonic should take it as
#Text=it exists in vegetables and fruit.
#Text=To this end he especially commends
#Text=spinach.
#Text=Dr.
#Text=Luff puts spinach first on a list of vegetables recommended to
#Text=those who suffer from gouty tendencies.
#Text=Spinach is very easily digested, and so juicy that no added water is
#Text=needed in which to cook it.
#Text=_Tomato._
#Text=
#Text=The tomato, according to an American physician, is one of the most
#Text=powerful _deobstruents_ (remover of disease particles, and opener of the
#Text=natural channels of the body) of the _materia medica_.
#Text=It should be used
#Text=in all affections of the liver, etc., where calomel is indicated.
#Text=The superstition that tomatoes are a cause of cancer is absolutely
#Text=without foundation.
#Text=Vegetarian cancer patients who have recovered after
#Text=being given up as "hopeless" by the orthodox faculty eat tomatoes
#Text=freely.
#Text=Another belief, strongly supported by some otherwise "advanced"
#Text=scientific men, is that tomatoes are bad for those who suffer from a
#Text=tendency to gout, or uric acid disease.
#Text=But this has been contradicted
#Text=by others.
#Text=The evil agency in the tomato is supposed to be the oxalic
#Text=salt which it undoubtedly contains.
#Text=But it has been shown by experiment
#Text=how certain chemical compounds as obtained from plants act quite
#Text=differently to the same compounds artificially prepared in the
#Text=laboratory.
#Text=So that the contention of those who assert that the tomato
#Text=is not only harmless, but even beneficial to gouty subjects, is not
#Text=unreasonable.
#Text=Speaking from experience, I can only say that one of the
#Text=goutiest subjects I know eats tomatoes nearly every day of his life, and
#Text=continues to progress rapidly towards health.
#Text=A tomato poultice is said to cleanse foul ulcers, and promote their
#Text=healing.
#Text=It should be renewed frequently, and applied hot.
#Text=_Turnip._
#Text=
#Text=Turnips are anti-scorbutic.
#Text=An old remedy for chronic coughs was turnip juice boiled with sugar.
#Text=The turnips were grated, the juice pressed out, and 2½ ozs. candied
#Text=sugar were allowed to 1 pint of juice.
#Text=This was boiled until it slightly
#Text=thickened.
#Text=A teaspoonful to be taken several times a day.
#Text=The green turnip tops, steamed until tender, are a good "spring
#Text=medicine."
#Text=_Thyme._
#Text=
#Text=The common garden thyme, used for flavouring, is credited with many
#Text=virtues.
#Text=It is said to inspire courage and enliven the spirits, and for
#Text=this reason should be taken by melancholy persons.
#Text=It is good against
#Text=nervous headache, flatulence, and hysterical affections.
#Text=It is
#Text=antiseptic.
#Text=_Walnuts._
#Text=
#Text=The walnut has been called vegetable arsenic because of its curative
#Text=value in eczema.
#Text=An oil obtained from the kernel has been found of great
#Text=service when applied externally in cases of skin diseases.
#Text=The leaves
#Text=of the walnut tree are also used for the same purpose, both externally
#Text=and internally.
#Text=One ounce of the leaves to 12 tablespoonfuls of boiling
#Text=water make a tea, half a tea-cup of which may be taken several times a
#Text=day.
#Text=The affected parts should also be washed with it.
#Text=Walnuts, to be well masticated, have been given to gouty and rheumatic
#Text=patients with great success.
#Text=About one dozen per day is the quantity
#Text=prescribed.
#Text=It is possible that herein lies the secret of the fact that
#Text=our ancestors invariably took walnuts with their wine.
#Text=The green, unripe walnut is useful for expelling worms.
#Text=_Wheat._
#Text=
#Text=Whole wheat is a perfect food.
#Text=In the form of white flour, however, it
#Text=is an imperfect, unbalanced food, on account of its deprivation of the
#Text=valuable phosphates which exist in the bran.
#Text=Rickets and malnutrition
#Text=generally are the outcome of the habitual use of white flour, unless the
#Text=loss of mineral matter is counter balanced by other foods.
#Text=Only the very finest wholemeal, such as "Artox," for example, should be
#Text=used for making bread, etc.
#Text=The ordinary coarse wholemeals are apt to
#Text=produce intestinal irritation.
#Text=_Cracked wheat_, soaked overnight in water and boiled for a couple of
#Text=hours, is a favourite prescription of American writers for habitual
#Text=constipation.
#Text=It may be obtained at most large "Food-Reform" stores.
#Text=_Bran Tea._
#Text=
#Text=Nervous or anæmic persons will derive great benefit from a course of
#Text=bran tea.
#Text=It is made as follows:--To every cup of bran allow 2 cups
#Text=distilled water.
#Text=Well wash the bran in cold water; it is generally full
#Text=of dust.
#Text=Put in a saucepan with the cold distilled water, cover tightly,
#Text=and boil for thirty minutes.
#Text=Strain, and flavour with sugar and lemon
#Text=juice to taste.
#Text=Take a teacupful night and morning.
